items.title: Authentication vs authorization
items.description: Authentication establishes who a principal is (identity proof), while authorization decides what that proven identity is permitted to do (access decision). They are distinct stages and must never be conflated: a valid login does not by itself grant access to any resource. In a Windows-auth platform, the OS/Negotiate handshake produces the authenticated identity, and the application's own RBAC and per-project rules produce every subsequent access decision.
items.applicability: Applies to every request path: each protected endpoint must first confirm a known authenticated identity, then run an explicit authorization check against the specific resource being touched.
items.example: A user authenticates via Windows Integrated Auth and is recognized as DOMAIN\jdoe. Opening project 42 still requires a separate authorization check that jdoe has a ProjectAccess row for project 42; authentication alone returns 401, missing project access returns 403.
items.limitation: Authentication confirms identity but says nothing about entitlement; treating a successful sign-in as an access grant is a common and serious flaw.

items.title: RBAC model
items.description: Role-Based Access Control assigns permissions to roles and assigns roles to users, so entitlements are managed through a small, auditable set of roles rather than per-user grants. This keeps the permission surface comprehensible, makes onboarding/offboarding a role assignment rather than a re-derivation of individual rights, and lets policy be reasoned about at the role level. Roles should map to job functions (e.g., Admin, Approver, Contributor, Viewer) and carry the minimum capabilities that function needs.
items.applicability: Use RBAC for coarse application-wide capabilities (administer users, approve knowledge, ingest, read-only); combine it with per-project access for the resource dimension.
items.example: An 'Approver' role can transition knowledge items from Pending to Approved across projects they can access, but cannot manage users; an 'Admin' role can manage users and roles but role membership, not a hand-edited permission list, is what grants the capability.
items.limitation: RBAC alone is coarse-grained; without a resource/project dimension it tends to over-grant, and an explosion of narrow roles ('role bloat') reintroduces the per-user complexity it was meant to remove.

items.title: Project-level access control
items.description: Beyond application roles, each project is an access boundary: a user must hold an explicit access grant to a project before any of that project's data (files, knowledge, profiles, chat) is readable or mutable. Every server-side query that returns project-scoped data must filter by the set of projects the caller is entitled to, not merely by the project id supplied in the request. This is the primary defense against Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR), where an attacker swaps an id in the URL or body to reach data they were never granted.
items.applicability: Applies to all controller actions and services that load or write project-scoped entities; the access check must run on the server using the authenticated identity, never trusting a client-supplied 'allowed project' value.
items.example: GET /Projects/42/Knowledge first confirms a ProjectAccess(user=jdoe, project=42) row exists; without it the action returns 403 and never executes the data query, so guessing /Projects/43 yields nothing.
items.limitation: Project-level checks are only effective if enforced on every entry point; one unguarded endpoint or report query that omits the project filter defeats the entire boundary.

items.title: User access matrix management
items.description: The effective entitlement of a user is the intersection of their role (what capabilities) and their project grants (over which resources), best understood as a user-by-project access matrix. Administering this matrix should be an explicit, reviewable operation: each cell (user, project) is granted or revoked deliberately, with the change recorded. Presenting the matrix to administrators makes over-grants visible and supports periodic recertification.
items.applicability: Use when designing admin screens and APIs for granting/revoking access; the matrix is the single source of truth for who can see which project and in what capacity.
items.example: An admin grid lists users down the side and projects across the top; toggling a cell writes a ProjectAccess row (or revokes it) and appends an audit entry recording actor, subject, project, and timestamp.
items.limitation: A matrix view aids comprehension but is not enforcement; the underlying server-side checks must read the same grant records the matrix displays, or the UI and reality diverge.

items.title: Role changes and role swap with segregation
items.description: Changing a user's role alters their capabilities immediately and must be treated as a privileged, audited action. Role swaps (moving from one role to another) should fully replace the prior capability set rather than accumulate, so privilege does not silently grow over time ('privilege creep'). Critically, the actor performing a role change should not be able to elevate themselves or bypass approval steps, preserving segregation between requesting and granting a privilege.
items.applicability: Applies to any administrative flow that assigns, swaps, or removes roles; enforce that self-elevation is blocked and that high-privilege grants require a distinct approver.
items.example: An admin demotes a departing lead from Approver to Viewer; the old Approver capabilities are removed atomically, the audit log records old role, new role, actor, and time, and an admin cannot grant themselves Admin without a second administrator's action.
items.limitation: Technical controls reduce but do not eliminate insider misuse; where a single super-admin exists, segregation must be supplemented by monitoring rather than enforced purely in code.

items.title: Least privilege
items.description: Every identity (human or service) should hold the minimum set of capabilities and resource grants needed to perform its function, and no more. Default new accounts to read-only or no access and add privileges only on demonstrated need, rather than starting broad and trimming later. Least privilege shrinks the blast radius of a compromised credential and limits the damage from honest mistakes.
items.applicability: Apply when defining roles, default account states, service-account scopes, and per-project grants; review grants whenever a user's job function changes.
items.example: A QA analyst who only needs to read one project's knowledge receives Viewer role and a single ProjectAccess row, not Contributor across all projects 'to be safe'.
items.limitation: Aggressive least privilege can generate friction and access-request churn; it is a control principle, not a substitute for an efficient grant/review workflow, and over-tightening can push users toward shadow workarounds.

items.title: Deny-by-default
items.description: Access decisions must fail closed: in the absence of an explicit grant, the answer is deny. New endpoints, new projects, and new users start with no access and become reachable only through a deliberate, recorded grant. This inverts the dangerous 'allow unless explicitly denied' posture, which leaves gaps wherever a denial rule was forgotten.
items.applicability: Apply to authorization middleware, route registration, and resource queries; an action with no authorization attribute or no project filter should be unreachable, not open.
items.example: A newly added controller action ships behind a global authorization policy that requires an explicit role/project match; if a developer forgets to annotate it, the default policy still denies anonymous and unentitled callers rather than exposing it.
items.limitation: Deny-by-default only holds if the framework's default is genuinely deny; a misconfigured fallback policy or an [AllowAnonymous] left in place silently reopens the door.

items.title: Segregation of duties
items.description: Segregation of Duties (SoD) splits a sensitive process across two or more people so no single actor can both initiate and approve a high-impact action. In a knowledge platform this typically means the author of a knowledge item cannot also be its approver, and the requester of a privilege cannot grant it to themselves. SoD is a control against both fraud and unilateral error, trading some speed for accountability.
items.applicability: Apply to approval lifecycles (author vs approver), privilege grants (requester vs grantor), and any state transition with material impact on data trust.
items.example: jdoe submits a business rule as Pending; the approve action checks that the approver's identity differs from the item's author and rejects self-approval, so a second reviewer must sign off.
items.limitation: SoD requires enough staff to separate the roles; in small teams it may be impractical and must be replaced by compensating controls such as after-the-fact review. This is a control summary, not compliance or legal advice.

items.title: Append-only audit trail
items.description: Security-relevant events (sign-in, access grant/revoke, role change, approval, secret access) must be recorded to an append-only audit trail that captures actor, subject, action, target, and timestamp. Append-only means existing entries are never updated or deleted through the application, so the log remains a trustworthy record even if other data is tampered with. The audit trail is what makes after-the-fact investigation, recertification, and accountability possible.
items.applicability: Apply to all privileged and access-changing operations; writes should go through a path that only inserts, and the table should not expose update/delete operations to the application surface.
items.example: Granting project 42 to jdoe inserts an immutable AuditEvent(actor=admin, action=GrantProjectAccess, target=project:42/user:jdoe, utc=...); a later revoke inserts a new row rather than editing the grant row.
items.limitation: Append-only at the application layer does not prevent a database administrator with direct table access from altering history; durable tamper-evidence requires DB-level controls or off-box log shipping. This summary is operational guidance, not a regulatory retention specification.

items.title: Service accounts
items.description: Non-interactive components (the app pool identity, background ingestion/health services, scheduled jobs) run under service accounts that must be scoped to exactly the resources they touch and never granted interactive or administrative human privileges. Each service account should be distinct and identifiable so its actions are attributable in the audit trail rather than masquerading as a human or sharing one omnibus account. Their credentials are managed as secrets, rotated, and never embedded in committed configuration.
items.applicability: Apply when configuring IIS app pool identity, hosted services, and any automated database or vector-store access; give each only the database rights and project scopes its function requires.
items.example: The ingestion background service connects to SQL with an account limited to the tables it writes; it cannot manage users, and its actions in the audit log are tagged with the service identity, not a person.
items.limitation: Service accounts are high-value targets because they are non-interactive and often broadly trusted; over-scoping one for convenience converts a single leaked credential into estate-wide exposure.

items.title: Disabled users handling
items.description: When a user is disabled (offboarded, suspended, or on leave) access must be cut promptly and completely: every authorization check must treat a disabled account as unentitled regardless of any roles or project grants still recorded against it. Disable rather than delete so the audit history and authored content remain attributable, but ensure the disabled flag is evaluated on the request path, not only hidden in the admin UI. Sessions and cached entitlements for the user should be invalidated so access does not survive the disable action.
items.applicability: Apply to authentication and authorization middleware and to any cached identity/role lookup; the disabled state must short-circuit access before resource queries run.
items.example: DOMAIN\jdoe leaves the team; setting User.IsDisabled=true causes the next request to fail authorization even though their Approver role and project grants still exist for audit purposes, and their authored items remain attributed to them.
items.limitation: If entitlements are cached, a disable can lag until the cache expires unless explicitly invalidated; relying solely on hiding the account in the UI leaves direct API and token access open.

items.title: Access reviews and recertification
items.description: Entitlements drift as people change roles and projects, so access must be reviewed on a periodic cadence and explicitly recertified or revoked by an accountable owner. Recertification answers 'does this person still need this?' for each role and project grant, catching privilege creep and orphaned access that no single grant decision would reveal. The review consumes the access matrix and the audit trail, and its outcomes (confirm or revoke) are themselves recorded.
items.applicability: Apply on a defined schedule (e.g., quarterly for sensitive projects) and on trigger events such as role changes or transfers; project owners or admins are the accountable reviewers.
items.example: Each quarter an admin is presented the access matrix per project; unconfirmed grants after the review window are flagged for revocation, and every confirm/revoke writes an audit entry.
items.limitation: Recertification is only as good as the reviewer's diligence; rubber-stamping ('approve all') defeats the control. This is an operational practice summary, not a prescription of any specific audit or regulatory framework.

items.title: Secrets handling and Data Protection keys
items.description: Secrets (API keys, connection-string credentials, encryption material) must never live in committed source or config; they belong in environment variables, a git-ignored local override, or a managed secret store. Sensitive values persisted by the application should be encrypted at rest, and the ASP.NET Core Data Protection key ring that performs that encryption must itself be protected: stored outside the repository, backed up, and consistent across all instances that need to decrypt. Losing or rotating these keys carelessly renders previously protected data undecryptable.
items.applicability: Apply to configuration, persistence of API keys, and any deployment with multiple web instances; the Data Protection keys directory must be persisted, access-restricted, and excluded from version control.
items.example: API keys are encrypted via Data Protection before being stored; the key ring lives in a git-ignored 'keys/' directory (or a shared protected path for a web farm) so all instances decrypt consistently, and connection strings with credentials come from environment variables, never committed appsettings.
items.limitation: Encryption at rest protects stored data but not a process that can already decrypt it; if the Data Protection keys are exposed or unprotected, the encryption provides little defense, and unsynchronized keys across instances cause intermittent decryption failures.
